title: Collisions: why momentum always survives but kinetic energy usually doesn't
scenario: A $m_1 = 2$ kg cart moving at $v_1 = 3$ m/s strikes a stationary $m_2 = 1$ kg cart. Slide the coefficient of restitution $e$ from $1$ (perfectly elastic — the carts bounce apart with no energy lost) down to $0$ (perfectly inelastic — they latch together). The **momentum** bars are the same total height before and after at *every* setting; the **kinetic-energy** bars match only when $e = 1$, and the gap that opens as $e \to 0$ is energy lost to deformation. Momentum is conserved because the contact forces are equal and opposite (Newton's third law); kinetic energy is not, because the deformation work need not come back.

assumptions.claim: An isolated 1D collision: no external horizontal force during contact, so the total momentum is conserved (the carts' mutual forces are internal).

The outcome is summarised by a single coefficient of restitution $e \in [0, 1]$ — the ratio of the relative speed of separation to the relative speed of approach — rather than modelling the contact force in detail.

steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you (two conservation rules)
steps.latex: m_1v_1+m_2v_2 = m_1v_1'+m_2v_2' \qquad (\text{elastic:})\ \ \tfrac12 m_1v_1^2+\tfrac12 m_2v_2^2 = \tfrac12 m_1v_1'^2+\tfrac12 m_2v_2'^2
steps.prose: Momentum is conserved in a collision, and for an *elastic* collision so is kinetic energy — two equations, two unknown final velocities. It works, but it is handed to you as a pair of rules, with no account of *why* momentum is always conserved while kinetic energy usually is not.

steps.label: Momentum conservation is the time-integral of Newton's third law
steps.latex: F_{12} = -F_{21} \ \Longrightarrow\ J_1 = \int F_{12}\,dt = -\int F_{21}\,dt = -J_2 \ \Longrightarrow\ \Delta p_1 = -\Delta p_2
steps.prose: During contact the two bodies push on each other with equal and opposite forces at every instant (Newton's third law). The impulse each receives is the time-integral of that force, $J=\int F\,dt$ — and since the forces are equal and opposite, so are the impulses. They cancel: $\Delta p_1+\Delta p_2=0$. The total momentum is unchanged no matter how complicated the contact force is, elastic or not. This is the impulse–momentum theorem doing the work the algebra states as a rule.

steps.label: Kinetic energy is different — the lost KE is the deformation work
steps.prose: The same contact force does work over each body's displacement, and those displacements differ, so the works do *not* cancel: energy goes into deforming the bodies. If they spring all the way back ($e=1$) it is returned and kinetic energy is conserved; otherwise the fraction $\tfrac12\mu(1-e^2)(\Delta v)^2$ stays as heat. The coefficient of restitution $e$ measures how much of the relative speed survives.

steps.label: Watch the two totals as you sweep $e$
steps.prose: Drag the restitution slider from $e=1$ down to $e=0$: the **momentum** total bar never moves (only the split between the two bodies shifts), while the **kinetic-energy** total bar shrinks, the gap shaded as energy lost. At $e=1$ both totals are conserved; at $e=0$ the bodies leave at the common velocity $v_{cm}$ and the KE loss is maximal.

proof.heading: Momentum is conserved in every collision (equal-and-opposite impulses cancel); kinetic energy is conserved only when the collision is perfectly elastic.
proof.detail: check $m_1v_1'+m_2v_2'=m_1v_1+m_2v_2$; check $v_2'-v_1'=e(v_1-v_2)$; check $\Delta\text{KE}=\tfrac12\mu(1-e^2)(\Delta v)^2$; $e=1\Rightarrow\text{KE}$ conserved; $e=0\Rightarrow$ common velocity $v_{cm}$

checks.claim: Total momentum is unchanged for *every* $e$: $m_1v_1'+m_2v_2' = m_1v_1+m_2v_2$. The contact forces are equal and opposite (Newton's third law), so the impulses $J=\int F\,dt$ cancel — true for any force profile, elastic or not.
The solved finals obey the restitution relation $v_2'-v_1' = e\,(v_1-v_2)$ — the relative speed of separation is $e$ times the relative speed of approach.
The kinetic energy lost is $\tfrac12\mu(1-e^2)(v_1-v_2)^2$ with reduced mass $\mu=m_1m_2/(m_1+m_2)$ — so KE is conserved only when $e=1$, and the loss is greatest when $e=0$.
At $e=1$ (perfectly elastic) the kinetic energy is unchanged: $\text{KE}_f = \text{KE}_i$.
At $e=0$ (perfectly inelastic) the bodies move off together at the common velocity $v_{cm}=(m_1v_1+m_2v_2)/(m_1+m_2)$.

misconception.claim: Momentum is only conserved when no kinetic energy is lost — so in a 'sticky' (inelastic) collision, momentum isn't conserved either.
misconception.refuted_by: Momentum is conserved in *every* collision, elastic or not. It follows from Newton's third law: during contact the two bodies push on each other with equal and opposite forces at every instant, so the impulses $J = \int F\,dt$ they receive are equal and opposite and cancel — the total momentum change is zero regardless of how much energy is lost. Kinetic energy is the quantity that is only *conditionally* conserved (when $e = 1$). The two laws are independent: drag $e$ to $0$ and watch the momentum total bar stay pinned while the kinetic-energy total bar collapses by $\tfrac12\mu(1-e^2)(\Delta v)^2$. People conflate the two because the *elastic* case happens to conserve both.
